# Show HN: Demoday — open-source clickable product demos, generated by your coding agent

---

**Post body:**

Hi HN,

I built Demoday, an open-source Claude Code skill that generates interactive product demos as self-contained HTML files. You install it once, ask your coding agent to run it, and it reads your codebase, generates one HTML file, and embeds it on your landing page with a single iframe.

**The problem:** Every early-stage founder needs a landing-page demo, and the options are bad. Screen recordings go stale the moment you change your UI. SaaS tools like Arcade and Navattic charge per-seat monthly fees and host your demo on their domain. Browser extensions capture pixel-perfect screenshots but produce rigid, non-interactive walkthroughs. Most founders just skip the demo entirely and lose visitors who churn before signing up.

**What Demoday does:** It generates a clickable, interactive demo from your source code. The output is one HTML file — pure HTML, CSS, and vanilla JS — that you embed with a single `<iframe>` tag. It works in Webflow, Framer, WordPress, Next.js, or anything else that serves static files. The demo lives in your git repo, reviewable via PR, and you update it by re-running the skill.

**How it works under the hood:**

1. The skill reads your repo: detects the framework, maps your real navigation structure by reading route files and sidebar components, and extracts brand colors from Tailwind config or CSS custom properties.
2. It generates a three-step carousel: step 1 is the payoff (your dashboard/outcome), step 2 is the core action, step 3 is the onboarding flow. Dark mode by default for contrast against light landing pages.
3. It writes one HTML file into `public/demos/` and embeds it with an iframe. The generated HTML includes a sidebar with your real nav labels, step controls, and realistic placeholder data.
4. Optionally, it records a 6-second 1080x1080 MP4 for X/Twitter using Puppeteer and ffmpeg.

**Use cases:**

- YC founders who need a landing-page demo before Demo Day and don't want to spend time on screen recordings
- Indie makers shipping with a coding agent who want to go from "no demo" to "clickable demo on landing page" in under a minute
- Product engineers who want their demo version-controlled and reviewable via PR instead of locked in a SaaS dashboard
- Open-source projects that want to show what the product does right on the README or docs site

**How we built it:**

Demoday is a Claude Code skill — a structured prompt with constraints that runs inside the coding agent. The skill itself is distributed as an npm package (`@demoday/skill`). When you run `npx @demoday/skill@latest init`, it writes the skill file to your local Claude Code skills directory. From there, the agent invokes it like any other skill.

The hardest part was making the generated HTML look good consistently across different products. We ended up with a canonical CSS template that ships verbatim with every demo — only CSS variables (colors) change. The sidebar never collapses, labels never truncate, and the layout uses fixed dimensions to avoid edge cases. It's intentionally rigid because consistency beats flexibility when an AI is writing the output.

The brand extraction pipeline checks Tailwind config, then CSS custom properties, then theme files, then shadcn config, and if nothing is found, it asks the user. Pricing and numbers are never invented — if they can't be sourced from the code, the skill asks.

**What surprised us most:**

1. **Navigation faithfulness matters more than visual fidelity.** Early versions tried to make the demo look exactly like the product. That was fragile and often wrong. The breakthrough was realizing that if the sidebar labels match the real product, users perceive the whole demo as "accurate" even if the content panels are simplified. The nav structure is the trust signal.

2. **Dark mode by default was a better product decision than we expected.** We defaulted to dark dashboards because they look better in social media clips and create contrast against light landing pages. But it also turned out that dark mode hides minor visual imperfections in AI-generated layouts. Light backgrounds are unforgiving; dark ones are more tolerant.

3. **One HTML file is the right distribution primitive.** We briefly considered generating React components, or a web component, or an npm package. All of those add framework assumptions and build steps. A single HTML file with inline CSS and JS works everywhere, is inspectable by anyone, diffs cleanly in git, and loads instantly. The simplest artifact won.

4. **AI-generated UI needs more constraints, not fewer.** Our best quality improvement came from making the CSS template completely fixed — no layout decisions left to the agent. The agent's job is filling in content and colors, not choosing spacing or font sizes. Every time we gave it more freedom, quality got worse.

**Pricing:** Free forever. No subscriptions, no per-seat, no per-project.

Install: `npx @demoday/skill@latest init`
Website: https://demoday.work
GitHub: https://github.com/emilankerwiik/demoday

Happy to answer questions about building skills for coding agents, AI-generated UI, or anything else.
